I'm a producer who wants to start releasing my own music. What do I need to learn? [Context: {"currentModule":"curriculum"}]

Producer → Artist learning path:

Phase 1 - IDENTITY (Week 1-2): Artist name, brand identity, visual direction. You know the craft but need the persona. Route to Brand.

Phase 2 - RELEASE MECHANICS (Week 3-4): Distribution setup, ISRC codes, metadata formatting. You know how to make music but not how to ship it. Route to Distribution.

Phase 3 - MARKETING FUNDAMENTALS (Week 5-8): Social media presence, content strategy, email list. Producers often skip this — it's the difference between releasing music and having it heard. Route to Marketing+Social.

Phase 4 - BUSINESS (Week 9-12): Publishing registration (BMI/ASCAP), LLC formation, split sheet templates. You probably know some of this from production credits but need to formalize. Route to Legal+Finance.

Phase 5 - LIVE PERFORMANCE (Month 4+): If applicable — DJ sets, live production, backing tracks. Route to Road Manager.

Your advantage: You already have the hardest skill (making great music). Everything else is learnable.

Someone mentioned I should register with a PRO but I have no idea what that means. Can you explain? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true}]

Running search_knowledge for PRO registration information. A PRO (Performing Rights Organization) collects performance royalties on your behalf — money generated when your songs are played publicly: radio, streaming services, live venues, TV, film, commercial use. ASCAP, BMI, and SESAC are the three main US PROs. As a songwriter, you need to join one and register your songs — otherwise you forfeit the performance royalties that are legally owed to you. I'll pull the full breakdown of how to register and what to expect.

My distributor is offering me a new contract with different royalty rates. Can you help me evaluate the specific terms they're sending over? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true}]

Evaluating specific contract terms is Legal's domain — they handle contract review, clause-by-clause analysis, and negotiation strategy for real agreements.

I can teach you the educational framework of what good distribution terms look like (royalty rates, reversion rights, exclusivity windows) — but for reviewing an actual contract with specific legal implications, Legal is the right team.

Should I build you a learning module on distribution contract fundamentals so you go into the Legal review more prepared, or would you like me to route you straight to Legal?

I'm doing well independently — 1M monthly listeners, making $60k/year from music. What should I be learning next to get to the next level? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"established","genre":"r&b"}}]

At 1M monthly and $60k, you've passed the survival stage — you're running a real business. The next level isn't about working harder, it's about building systems and unlocking revenue you're currently leaving on the table. Building your path with `create_learning_path`.

**Revenue you're probably missing right now:**
- Neighboring rights (international): If you haven't registered with PPL (UK) or a neighboring rights agency, there's international performance royalty money sitting uncollected. At your stream volume, this could be $3-8k/year.
- Sync licensing: Your catalog is sync-ready but no one's pitching it. A sync agent or library placement could add $10-50k/year in one-time fees.
- YouTube Content ID monetization: Are you collecting on UGC (fan videos using your music)? If not, that's passive revenue you're losing.

**Structural upgrades for the $100k+ level:**
- Team building: At 1M listeners, you need at minimum a booking agent (for live revenue) and a publicist (for press/visibility cycles around releases). These are commission-based — they don't cost you upfront.
- International sub-publishing: If you're collecting royalties only in the US, you're missing 40-60% of the global pie.
- Direct-to-fan conversion: Move 1-2% of your Spotify listeners to your email list (10,000-20,000 emails). That list is worth more than playlists — it's audience you own.

**Tax and business optimization:**
- S-Corp election: At $60k, you may save $5-8k/year in self-employment tax by electing S-Corp status and paying yourself a reasonable salary
- Deduction optimization: Home studio, equipment, travel, streaming service subscriptions, co-writing sessions — all legitimate deductions

Each of these modules is available for deep-dive. Which one do you want to tackle first?

I want to start playing shows and touring. I've never booked a show before. Where do I start? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true}]

Touring 101 for first-time performers — the real starting point:

**Start local, build your case**
Before approaching venues, you need: (1) recorded music or a strong social presence to share, (2) a performance-ready set (even 20–30 minutes), (3) ideally some local audience who will show up

**Finding your first shows:**
- **Open mics** — zero pressure, meet the room, understand the venue before you headline it
- **Support slots** — reach out to artists in your city who play similar music and ask to open for them. You bring their audience value.
- **DIY shows** — rent a community space, gallery, or bar back room. You control the show and keep all door revenue.
- **Venue pitching** — once you have a few shows under your belt and can demonstrate an audience, start pitching venue bookers

**What a venue needs from you:**
- A short pitch (who you are, your music, your draw — be honest about draw size)
- Links: music + social
- A show deposit or guarantee structure (venues either offer a guarantee, or you work door deal percentage)

**On the road (regional touring):**
- Book a geographic string — minimize dead miles between cities
- Use Songkick/Bandsintown to promote once booked
- Routing apps: Google Maps works; RouteNote and similar tools for multi-city routing

**Your real job:** Build a local audience first. Touring to empty rooms in other cities is expensive practice. Touring to engaged audiences is an investment.

Want me to build a learning path on venue booking and tour planning?

The system produces the same style regardless of the prompt. [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true}]

Classic overfitting symptom — the model has collapsed to a single mode.

**Curriculum Intervention:**
1. Design high-diversity task sets (wildly different styles, genres, aesthetics)
2. Force exploration over exploitation (reward novelty in scoring)
3. Temperature check (ensure generation parameters aren't locked)
4. Re-introduce adversarial/contradictory prompts to break the pattern

Goal: Restore output diversity while maintaining quality floor.

I'm 16. Are there special things I need to know about the music business as a minor? [Context: {"currentModule":"curriculum"}]

Important considerations for minor artists:

1. CONTRACTS: Minors generally can't sign binding contracts. A parent/guardian must co-sign, but even then, some contracts can be voided. This is actually PROTECTION, not a limitation.

2. COOGAN LAW: In some states, a percentage of a minor's entertainment earnings must be set aside in a protected account. Know your state's laws.

3. DISTRIBUTION: Most distributors require you to be 18+ or have a parent create the account.

4. SOCIAL MEDIA: COPPA restrictions apply under 13. At 16, most platforms are fine but parental awareness is important.

5. EXPLOITATION PROTECTION: Never sign long-term contracts as a minor. Labels sometimes offer 'deals' to minors precisely because they're easier to take advantage of.

6. SCHOOL BALANCE: The industry will wait. Education won't harm your career — many successful artists released their first music in their 20s.

Route to Legal for minor-specific contract review and Finance for Coogan account setup.

How do I network in the music industry when I don't know anyone? [Context: {"currentModule":"curriculum"}]

Industry networking — the skill nobody teaches:

1. START LOCAL: Open mic nights, local shows, producer meetups. The person next to you at a small show today could be a playlist curator tomorrow.

2. PROVIDE VALUE FIRST: Don't lead with 'check out my music.' Lead with 'I loved your set, can I share it on my socials?' Relationships start with giving.

3. ONLINE NETWORKING:
- Twitter/X music communities
- Discord servers for your genre
- Reddit music production subreddits
- Comment genuinely on other artists' posts (not 'fire 🔥' — actual engagement)

4. COLLABORATION > COLD OUTREACH: 'Let's make something together' is 10x more effective than 'can you help me.'

5. INDUSTRY EVENTS: SXSW, A3C, CMJ, local music conferences. The hallway conversations are more valuable than the panels.

6. FOLLOW UP: Met someone interesting? Follow up within 48 hours. 95% of networking value is lost because nobody follows up.

7. LONG GAME: Industry relationships compound over years, not days. Stay in touch without always asking for something.

I've been approached by a small label and I want to understand what I'm getting into. Where do I start learning? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true}]

Running create_learning_path for label deal preparation. Before signing anything, you need to understand the core mechanics: (1) Recoupment — how an advance works and when (if ever) you start receiving royalties; (2) Royalty rates — what percentage of your recorded music revenue goes to you vs. the label; (3) 360 deals — whether the label takes a share of touring, merch, or sync income; (4) Term and options — how long you're committed and how many albums they control; (5) Your pre-deal leverage — what you can negotiate and what you can't as a new artist.

This is a 5-module intermediate path. I'll route the actual contract review to Legal — this curriculum gives you the knowledge to have an informed conversation with your attorney.

Teach me how social media actually works for musicians. [Context: {"currentModule":"curriculum"}]

Social media for musicians — the education most artists need:

1. PLATFORMS: Focus on 2 max. TikTok (discovery engine) + Instagram (fan relationship). Spreading across 5 platforms = weak everywhere.

2. ALGORITHM TRUTH: Algorithms reward consistency and engagement, not talent. Posting 3x/week beats posting once with a masterpiece.

3. CONTENT RATIO (80/20): 80% value/entertainment, 20% promotion. Nobody follows artists who only post 'stream my new single.'

4. WHAT WORKS: Behind-the-scenes, process videos, personality content, tutorials, reaction content, collaboration clips. NOT: professional photos with captions like 'link in bio.'

5. ENGAGEMENT > FOLLOWERS: 5,000 engaged fans > 50,000 passive followers. Respond to comments, DMs, build community.

6. CONVERSION: Social media → email list → direct relationship. Own your audience; don't rent it from platforms.

Route to Social for hands-on strategy execution once you understand these principles.

I released music 8 years ago and took a break. The industry has completely changed. Help me catch up. [Context: {"currentModule":"curriculum"}]

Welcome back. Here's what changed:

1. STREAMING DOMINANCE: Physical sales → downloads → streaming. Revenue is per-stream, not per-purchase. Catalog matters more than ever.
2. DISTRIBUTION DEMOCRATIZED: You don't need a label to be on every platform. DistroKid/TuneCore/$20/year.
3. SOCIAL MEDIA = MARKETING: TikTok didn't exist 8 years ago and now drives more music discovery than radio.
4. DIRECT-TO-FAN: Patreon, Bandcamp, email lists. The middlemen are optional.
5. VISUAL CONTENT: Music without visual content barely exists. Every release needs visual strategy.

GOOD NEWS: Your catalog is still earning (or should be). Your past work gives you credibility new artists don't have.

ACTION PLAN: Claim all existing profiles (Spotify for Artists, Apple Music for Artists). Audit where your old music lives. Then we'll build a relaunch strategy. Route to Distribution for profile audit.

I want to design a 6-week online music production bootcamp for intermediate producers. What should the progression look like? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"developing","genre":"electronic"}}]

A 6-week bootcamp for intermediate producers should assume DAW literacy and build toward a finished, release-ready track.

**Week 1: Sound Design & Synthesis**
- Subtractive, FM, wavetable, and granular synthesis
- Building sounds from init patches (don't use presets)
- Sampling and resampling techniques
- Deliverable: Create a custom sound palette of 10 original sounds

**Week 2: Arrangement & Composition**
- Song structure analysis (deconstructing reference tracks)
- Building tension and release (drops, builds, breakdowns)
- Countermelody and harmonic layering
- Deliverable: Full arrangement of a track with at least 4 distinct sections

**Week 3: Mixing Fundamentals**
- Gain staging and signal flow
- EQ: subtractive before additive, frequency scouting
- Compression: types, attack/release, parallel compression
- Deliverable: Mix your Week 2 arrangement to a balanced stereo bus

**Week 4: Advanced Mixing & Effects**
- Spatial design: reverb, delay, modulation as creative tools
- Automation: volume rides, filter sweeps, panning movement
- Mid/side processing and stereo imaging
- Deliverable: Polished mix with automation and spatial depth

**Week 5: Mastering**
- The mastering chain: EQ → compression → limiter → metering
- LUFS targets per platform (Spotify: -14, Apple: -16, YouTube: -13, Club: -6)
- Stem mastering vs stereo mastering
- A/B referencing against commercial releases
- Deliverable: Master your track to streaming specifications

**Week 6: Release Preparation & Portfolio**
- File delivery: WAV, MP3, codec considerations
- Metadata: ISRC registration, credits, genre tagging
- Cover art integration and visual-audio coherence
- Portfolio presentation: how to present production work professionally
- Final deliverable: Release-ready track with metadata, cover art, and distribution-ready files

**Assessment:** Peer review of final tracks + instructor feedback on technical execution and creative vision.

There's so much to learn I don't even know where to begin. I feel paralyzed. [Context: {"currentModule":"curriculum"}]

Take a breath. You don't need to know everything at once.

Start with exactly ONE thing:

1. Do you have ONE finished song? If no → finish one song. Everything else is premature.
2. Have a finished song? → Get it on streaming platforms (Distribution). This alone teaches you 80% of what you need.
3. Song is live? → Tell people about it (Marketing basics + Social).

That's it. Song → Distribution → Tell People. Everything else (publishing, legal, merch, touring) comes AFTER you've done the basics. The music industry rewards action over knowledge. The artist who releases imperfectly beats the artist who studies endlessly.

I'll build your curriculum one step at a time. We're not climbing the mountain today — we're taking the first step.

I've never performed live. What do I need to know? [Context: {"currentModule":"curriculum"}]

Live performance crash course:

1. SETLIST: 30-45 minutes for opening, 60-90 for headline. Open strong, close stronger. Put your weakest song third.

2. PREPARATION: Rehearse your set 10+ times before your first show. Know your songs cold — nerves will steal 30% of your ability.

3. SOUNDCHECK:
- Arrive early. Be polite to sound engineer — they control your show.
- Check your monitor mix carefully. If you can't hear yourself, you'll perform badly.

4. STAGE PRESENCE:
- Eye contact with different sections of the audience
- Move. Static performers lose energy.
- Talk between songs. Connection > perfection.

5. TECH PREP:
- Backing tracks: Run from a laptop with a backup on phone
- ALWAYS bring: XLR cable, guitar cable, power strip, setlist taped to floor
- Battery check on everything wireless

6. POST-SHOW: Be at the merch table. Fans remember artists who make time for them.

Route to Road Manager for booking shows and venue logistics.
